coredata.dc:title: Dual sensing of tetracycline and l-Lysine using green synthesized carbon dots from Nigella sativa seeds
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Neha
Yun, Kyusik
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) that exhibit bright-blue fluorescence were hydrothermally synthesized from Nigella sativa seeds and employed for selective and quantitative detection of tetracycline (TC) and l-lysine (L-Lys). Specifically, TC addition led to quenching of CDs’ fluorescence intensity, while the enhanced fluorescence was spotted in the presence of L-Lys. CD quenching was attributed to both dynamic and static mechanisms, which is different from earlier-reported studies. In contrast, the introduction of L-Lys supported the emergence of hydrogen bonds between CDs and L-Lys, and consequently resulted in an increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity. PL quenching was linearly correlated with TC concentration ranged from 0.01 to 40 μM with 14 nM detection limit. A linear correlation was observed for L-Lys in the concentration range of 0–500 μM and displayed a limit of detection of 453 nM. CDs showed TC detection in the range of 95.2–102.5% in tap water, river water, urine, and powdered milk. Likewise, CDs have been successfully utilized for detecting L-Lys in serum samples with a recovery range of 98.9–100.3%. The study describes the use of a dual sensing strategy for TC and L-Lys using green synthesized CDs. This study will provide an in-depth insight into the mechanism of TC and L-Lys detection by CDs, which will help to understand the role of different nanoparticles in sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nigella sativa
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal
Tetracycline
l-lysine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective detection of TC and L-Lys
Real sample analysis of TC and L-Lys
Determination of TC via fluorescence quenching
Determination of L-Lys via fluorescence enhancement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CDs synthesis from Nigella sativa seeds and sensing of TC and L-Lys.

The optical properties of CDs. (A) The absorption and normalized emission spectra of CD (inset: CD solution under (i) 365 nm UV light lamp and (ii) white light). (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of CDs for varying excitation wavelengths from 290 nm to 450 nm. Morphology analysis of CDs. (C) TEM image of the CDs, inset HRTEM image. (D) The size distribution of the CDs.
(A) FTIR spectra of a) Nigella sativa seeds extract and b) synthesized CDs, (B) XPS survey, (C) C1s spectra, (D) N1s spectra and, (E) O1s spectra of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with various concentrations of tetracycline (TC) (0.01, 0.05, 0.200, 0.600, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 40, 80, 120, 160, 200, 240, 280, and 320 μM). (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) of CDs with various concentrations of TC (Insert-linear relationship between I0/I and the concentration of TC in the range of 0.01–40 μM). (C) Bar graph selectivity study of the CDs displaying the change in fluorescent intensities (I0–I) with reference to Io over potential interferents (Uric acid, glucose, ascorbic acid, kanamycin, neomycin, ciprofloxacin, amikacin, and tobramycin at 100 μM concentration) for the TC (20 μM) detection. Where, I0 and I represent the fluorescent intensities in the absence and presence of as-synthesized CDs, respectively.

The mechanisms of tetracycline (TC) detection by CDs (A) The normalized maximum excitation (a), emission (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs and UV spectrum of TC. (B) Suppressed efficiency (E%) of corrected (red) and observed (black) measurements for CDs after addition of different concentrations of TC, E = 1- F/F0 , Where, F and F0 are the steady-state fluorescence intensities of CDs in the presence and absence of TC. (C) Temperature-dependent PL of the CDs in the presence of TC. (D) UV spectra of CDs (a), TC (b) and CDs in the presence of TC (c). (E) The energy diagrams of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMOs) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMOs) for CDs and TC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with various concentrations of L-Lys (0.00, 1, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250,300, 350, 400, 450, and 500 μM). (B) Plot of fluorescent intensity ratio (F0/F) versus the logarithm concentrations of L-Lys (0.0–500 μM), where F0 represents in the absence and F in the presence of L-Lys. (C) Selectivity study of the CDs over potential interferents (glycine, valine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, serine, asparagine, glutamine, glutamic acid, arginine, histidine, alanine, methionine, threonine, proline, aspartic acid, tryptophan, and cysteine at 10 mM concentration) for the L-Lys (100 μM) detection with corresponding bar graph. (D) UV spectra of CDs (a), L-Lys (b) and CDs in the presence of L-Lys (c).
The interaction mechanism of CDs with L-Lys.

head.ce:title: Dual sensing of tetracycline and l-Lysine using green synthesized carbon dots from Nigella sativa seeds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Gyeonggi-do, 13120, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Gyeonggi-do, 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) that exhibit bright-blue fluorescence were hydrothermally synthesized from Nigella sativa seeds and employed for selective and quantitative detection of tetracycline (TC) and l-lysine (L-Lys). Specifically, TC addition led to quenching of CDs’ fluorescence intensity, while the enhanced fluorescence was spotted in the presence of L-Lys. CD quenching was attributed to both dynamic and static mechanisms, which is different from earlier-reported studies. In contrast, the introduction of L-Lys supported the emergence of hydrogen bonds between CDs and L-Lys, and consequently resulted in an increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity. PL quenching was linearly correlated with TC concentration ranged from 0.01 to 40 μM with 14 nM detection limit. A linear correlation was observed for L-Lys in the concentration range of 0–500 μM and displayed a limit of detection of 453 nM. CDs showed TC detection in the range of 95.2–102.5% in tap water, river water, urine, and powdered milk. Likewise, CDs have been successfully utilized for detecting L-Lys in serum samples with a recovery range of 98.9–100.3%. The study describes the use of a dual sensing strategy for TC and L-Lys using green synthesized CDs. This study will provide an in-depth insight into the mechanism of TC and L-Lys detection by CDs, which will help to understand the role of different nanoparticles in sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic used for treating animals, growth promoter for livestock, and also utilized as human medicine [1]. Owing to its non-biodegradable nature, its detrimental remnants typically remain on the soil's surface or in drinking and groundwater. Hence, the excessive use of TC causes adverse effects to human health and the environment. In humans, the abuse of TC leads to hepatic damage, kidney toxicity, allergic reactions, gastrointestinal reactions, and yellowing of the teeth [2]. In addition, overuse of antibiotics promotes bacterial resistance, leading to changes in the environmental microflora [3]. By 2012, in the United States and the European Union, the expected consumption of TC was 5954.36 and 113.2 tons, respectively [4]. As per European Union Law, the presence of TC in milk should be below 100 ng/mL, which necessitates the development of a sensitive and instant method for the detection of TC in milk, water, and other food products [5]. Until now, several investigative methods have been employed for TC identification, including fluorescence-based methods [6], immunoassays, capillary electrophoresis (CE) [7], mass spectrometry [8], high-pressure liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) [9], and electrochemical sensors [10]. However, most of the methods need a skilled person, sophisticated equipment and laborious sample preparation. Liu et al. described various types of TCs sensors and their mechanisms in a recent review article [10].

l-Lysine (L-Lys, an indispensable amino acid) is employed as a marker to assess the nutritional quality of food and pharmaceutical preparations [11]. It is commonly used as a human nutritional additive and as a supplement in animal feedstocks [12]. The estimated average intake in humans is 4.0–5.0 g per day [13]. The deficiency of L-Lys in humans may result in medical complications, such as fatigue, anemia, hair loss, distorted and stunted growth, inflamed eyes, and reproductive complications [14]. However, a high amount of L-Lys in blood or urine can be an indication of metabolic problems, such as hyperlysinemia [15] or cystinuria [16]. In Alzheimer's disease, L-Lys plays a significant role in the neurotoxicity of αβ oligomers [17]. Monitoring and determining L-Lys concentrations is highly desirable. Several analytical approaches for determining L-Lys concentrations in biological fluids include chromatography [18], spectroscopy [19], capillary electrophoresis, flow injection chemiluminescence [20], and amperometric biosensors [21]. Unfortunately, these methods are tedious, time-consuming, require trained personnel, and use expensive reagents and equipment [22]. Therefore, it is of interest to create a fast, simple and economic strategy for L-Lys detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) are spherical nanoparticles that are less than 10 nm in size and have evoked considerable interest among researchers since their discovery in 2004 [23]. The attractive properties of CDs include their high chemical stability, water solubility, photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, exceptional biocompatibility, low cost, and facile synthesis and modification [[24],[25]]. A growing body of literature has revealed the importance of using green synthesis for CDs from natural and renewable materials for their facile accessibility, cleanliness, non-toxicity, and economic production [[25]]. Two main approaches have been exploited for the fabrication of CDs: top-down and bottom-up [25]. CDs are promising materials in multidisciplinary research fields such as drug delivery, biological labeling, biosensors, antimicrobial coatings, metal ion sensing, and gene delivery [24,65]. To identify TC, CDs based on rice residue [2], xylan [26], o-phenylenediamine [27], tobacco [28], citric acid [29–32], durian shell [33], fungus fibers [34], ascorbic acid [35], glycerol [36] have been used for TC detection. On the other hand, for the detection of L-Lys, o-phenylenediamine [37] and CQDs using ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) with l-cysteine [38], have been reported. However, there is no report on the dual sensing of TC and L-Lys using CDs from natural precursors.

In this paper, we report CDs prepared from Nigella sativa seeds that can detect TC and L-Lys. The seeds are commonly known as black seeds or black cumin. CDs have been prepared hydrothermally without the use of any chemical additives. The suppression of fluorescence intensity was observed after adding TC, while L-Lys addition showed an enhanced fluorescence in as-synthesized CDs. Moreover, the dual-sensing mechanisms of CDs are also investigated and discussed.

Nigella sativa seeds were purchased from a nearby market in India. TC, kanamycin, ciprofloxacin, neomycin, amikacin, and tobramycin were kindly provided by Lenus Life Care Solan, Himachal Pradesh, India. Oxytetracycline (O-TC), L-Lys, glycine, valine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, serine, asparagine, glutamine, glutamic acid, arginine, histidine, alanine, methionine, threonine, proline, aspartic acid, tryptophan, cysteine, ascorbic acid, uric acid, trichloroacetic acid, sodium hydroxide, glucose, and serum were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultrapure Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩcm) (Samwoo S &T Co., Ltd, Korea) was used for all experiments.

Varian Cary Bio 50 spectrophotometer (California, USA) was utilized to obtain UV–Vis spectra. All fluorescent data measurements were conducted on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Varian Cary eclipse, USA) with a 20 nm bandpass difference in excitation and emission wavelengths. Sizes and morphological details of CDs were measured using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Titan 80–300, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were acquired using PHI 5000 Versa Probe (Ulvac-PHI) spectrometer (Japan) with a monochromator Al Kα (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source. A Jasco FT/IR-4000, 6000 series spectrometer (Japan) was utilized to record the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra.

Carbon dots were synthesized via a low-cost hydrothermal method using Nigella sativa seeds as the carbon precursor. Nigella sativa was first ground and mixed with distilled water. The mixture was poured into a Teflon reaction vessel, sealed, and autoclaved at 120 °C for 12 h. After heating, the autoclave was cooled. The solution of as-prepared CDs was filtered (0.22 μm filter) and viewed under UV light to observe fluorescence. The supernatant was used to characterize the CDs using UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, XPS, TEM, and high-resolution (HR) TEM. The remaining material was stored at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C) for further use. The effect of pH and UV irradiation was evaluated for as-synthesized CDs.

Carbon dot quantum yield was measured following an already established procedure. Quinine sulfate was used as the reference standard for study. The calculation of the QY was conducted using equation (1):
(1) QX = QSD(IX/ISD) (ASD/AX),
where Q is the QY, I represent fluorescence emission intensity, and A is the absorbance. SD and X indicate the standard and sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of TC and L-Lys
ce:section.ce:para: For a standard run, 2 mL of the CD solution was mixed with distilled water. Various concentrations of TC and L-Lys solutions were added, such that the final volume was 2.5 mL. Different concentrations of TC and L-Lys solutions were mixed with CD solution (2 mL) and the final volume was adjusted with water to 2.5 mL. After mixing well, the reaction mixture was allowed to react for 1 min, followed by fluorescence measurement. Stock solutions of kanamycin, ciprofloxacin, neomycin, amikacin, tobramycin, glucose, uric acid, and ascorbic acid were prepared and incubated with CDs to determine the selectivity of TC detection. Similarly, stock solutions of the remaining nineteen amino acids were prepared and tested to determine the selectivity of L-Lys detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis of TC and L-Lys
ce:section.ce:para: For detection in real samples, water was taken from the Tancheon river (Korea) and tap. Urine was collected from a healthy volunteer and powdered milk was procured from a local market. The urine was diluted by a factor of 10 before use. Powdered milk was pretreated following a previously reported procedure with slight modifications [39]. Powdered milk (2 g) was mixed with 15 mL of 61 mM trichloroacetic acid, sonicated for 20 min, stirred for 10 min, and followed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm/20 min). Sodium hydroxide was used to adjust the pH of the treated sample to 6.8. The river water, tap water, urine, and milk samples, were tested at three TC concentrations (12, 24, and 36 μM). For L-Lys analysis, serum was diluted by 100 × and spiked with different concentrations of L-Lys (100, 250, and 400 μM). The same detection procedure was used as stated above.

Fig. 2A depicts the absorption band and the maximum emission band of CDs. Two peaks of absorption were visible at approximately at 272.8 and 324 nm, which were accredited to transitions of π – π* (C=C bond) and the n–π* (C=O group), respectively [40]. The as-prepared CDs showed a strong blue emission at 406.2 nm when excited at 330 nm, demonstrating the typical luminescent behavior of CDs. The as-prepared CDs showed a blue fluorescence with a QY of 8%. Emission spectra were recorded at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2B), which could be owing to existing surface defects and different sized particles. With increasing excitation wavelengths (290–450 nm), a shift in the emission spectra wavelengths was witnessed from 405.4 to 500 nm.

The as-synthesized CDs were analyzed with TEM to determine the morphological features. As per TEM image in Fig. 2C, CDs are spherical and highly dispersed. HRTEM determined the spacing distance of carbon lattice fringe to be 0.27 nm (Fig. 2C inset). The Nigella sativa CDs had an average diameter of 4 nm (Fig. 2D). To thoroughly investigate the functional groups of CDs, the FTIR spectra were collected. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, CDs distinguish by several peaks compared to raw Nigella sativa seeds. The absorption bands at 3008 and 2927 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H and C–H stretching vibrations, respectively [41]. Similarly, vibrations (stretching) in CO, CC and NH2 bonds led to peaks at 1651 cm−1 and 1544 cm−1, respectively [28]. The bands at 1244, 1488, and 1102 cm−1 are absorption vibrations of C–N, C–H and C–N/C–O, respectively. These findings suggest that the CDs exhibit H, O, and C groups that are inherited from their source material and make the CDs water-soluble.

XPS was conducted to explore the CD surface groups. The XPS reveal that CDs are composed of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), and oxygen (O1s) binding energy peaks at 287, 402, and 534 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3B. The CDs are comprised of 67.86% carbon, 10% nitrogen, and 22.42% oxygen. The high resolution C1s XPS data exhibit three peaks at 285.4, 287.3, and 289.5 eV, ascribe to the C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 3C) [42].

Three peaks are shown in the high resolution N1s spectra, which are located at 400.1, 401.3, 402.2, and 403 eV, depicting the C= N, N-(C)3, C–NH, and a charging effect, respectively (Fig. 3D) [43]. The presence of the CN bond indicates that the CDs are formed through a condensation.

Reaction (Schiff base) between the aldehyde and amine groups, which further demonstrates the existence of active hydroxyl and amine functional groups [27]. As shown in Fig. 3E, the O1s fitting peak depicted two peaks at 533.5 and 530.7 eV, assigned to OC–O and O–H groups, respectively [42]. The results obtained from XPS are comparable to the FTIR results and confirm that the CDs contain numerous functional groups that are responsible for their excellent water solubility and PL properties [44].

The stability of as-prepared CDs at different pH values (acidic to basic) was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S1a, the CD PL was enhanced as the pH increased, with the lowest intensity demonstrated in acidic media and the highest intensity at an alkaline pH. At a pH of 4, the PL intensity drastically changed and exhibited a sharp increase at a pH above 9, suggesting the pH-dependent PL behavior of CDs. This variation could be ascribed to the ionization of surface hydroxyl groups (–OH), affecting the confinement energy levels of CDs, responsible for fluorescence modulation. Our results agree with those previously reported in the literature. The optimum pH value of 7.0 was selected for all subsequent applications.

To explore the photostability, the CDs were exposed to UV light (365 nm) for 60 min, and the emission profiles were collected. The PL intensity of the CDs remained unaffected after UV treatment for 1 h, demonstrating the photostability of the as-synthesized CDs (Fig. S1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC via fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: We demonstrate that the as-synthesized CDs selectively sense TC in solution. The effect of pH (3–10) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/TC was checked to optimize TC detection. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the intensity of CDs remained nearly similar across the entire pH range, which benefited the reliable sensing system [36].

The abundance of –OH groups on TC and –COOH groups on the CD surface enables TC-CD interactions [28]. Fig. 4 A shows that the CD fluorescence is quenched when TC is added, as previously reported [27]. The quenched blue color intensity can be observed in the inserted pictures of the vials (Fig. S3) after adding different concentrations of TC (0–36 μM) in distilled water and excited at 365 nm. The CD fluorescent emission wavelength was red-shifted in the presence of TC from 406 to 419 nm. In this “turned off” mode, changes in the fluorescence intensity (I0/I, where I0 is the fluorescence intensity value without TC, and I depicts the fluorescence intensity value with TC) displayed a linear relationship with TC concentrations ranged from 0.01 to 40 μM (Fig. 4B) with a correlation coefficient 0.9994 (Fig. 4B inset). The linear regression equation was I0/I = 0.02058x+1.03781 and the achieved limit of detection (LOD) was 14 nM. Compared to previous reported CD-based detection methods, Nigella sativa CDs offered an excellent detection range and.
A low LOD. The LOD calculations were done by using 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of CD fluorescence intensity and k denotes slope [2,66].

Notably, CDs prepared from Nigella sativa seeds do not require additives and can potentially be used for the direct sensing of TC. CD selectivity was investigated (TC at 20 μM) by measuring the PL in the presence of antibiotics (kanamycin, neomycin, ciprofloxacin, amikacin, and tobramycin) and other interferents commonly found in the environment, such as uric acid, glucose, and ascorbic acid at 100 μM concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4C, the test substances showed negligible interference on the PL intensity, indicating that the CDs have a high selectivity for TC (Fig. S4). Finally, to verify the feasibility of the CDs for TC detection, solutions of TC at 12, 24, and 36 μM in tap water, river water, urine, and powdered milk were tested. The results for TC determination are summarized in Table S1. The detection of TC ranged from 95.2% to 102.5%, signifying a high accuracy for real sample analysis.

To expose the mechanism of CDs quenching for TC sensing, spectral evaluation and fluorescence lifetime assays were conducted. There was good spectral lap over between the absorption and excitation wavelengths of TC and CD, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 5 A. This indicates the quenching may occur by the inner filter effect (IFE) [36,45]. To gain better insights on TC detection based on IFE, the molar extinction coefficient (ε) of TC was calculated. Compared to all interferents, when measured at the excitation wavelength (330 nm) of CDs, the value ε for TC (ε = 9110) was found to be very high, resulting in good IFE performance (Table S2 and Fig. S5). To demonstrate the involvement of IFE in the quenching of PL intensity of CDs, the following equation (2) was used [46]:
(2)
F cor
F obs
=
2.3
dA ex
1 −
10
−
dA ex
.
2.3
sA em
10
gA em
1 −
10
−
sA em
Where Fcor represents PL intensity after IFE correction and Fobs is the observed PL intensity. Aex and Aem represent absorbance of CDs at 330 and 406 nm, respectively, after adding TC (Fig. S6). d, s and g, represents the cuvette width (1 cm), excitation beam thickness (0.1 cm) and distance from the edge of the excitation beam to the cuvette edge (0.4 cm), respectively. The Fobs, is the observed PL intensity of CDs and Fcor, is the corrected PL intensity by eliminating IFE from observed PL intensity of CDs using equation (2). The correction factor (CF) of the IFE (Fcor/Fobs), the corrected percentage (Ecor %) and the measured percentage (Eobs%) of fluorescence quenching efficiencies were evaluated at each of the different concentrations of TC as shown in Table S3. Fig. 5B depicts the suppressed efficiency (E%) of the Ecor and Eobs for CDs in the presence of TC. We concluded that the IFE quenching efficiency of TC to CDs reaches up to 35%, suggesting the coexistence of another quenching mechanism.

In order to further investigate the role of IFE in the detection mechanism, one model compound was studied, namely O-TC, identical to TC except for having a hydroxyl group at B ring's position ‘5.’ The UV spectra of O-TC showed good overlap with the excitation spectra of CDs, similar to TC (Fig. S7). The quenching efficiency of TC was found to be little more than O-TC, comparatively indicating that IFE is more in TC than O-TC (correlation coefficient 0.9982) (Figs. S8 and S9) [47].

In addition, PL quenching could be due to the dynamic quenching (DQ) or static quenching (SQ), alone or in combination. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method was applied to evaluate the excitation observance of CDs without and with TC. The addition of TC lowers the CD lifetime from 13.76 to 4.98 ns (Fig. S10), which implicates the dynamic quenching mechanism [48].
To analyze the PL quenching mechanism further, the effect of different temperatures (20 °C, 40 °C and 50 °C) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of TC was assessed.

Increasing the temperature increases the quenching constant (Fig. 5C), which could be due to a surge in collisions between CDs and TC [48]. Since F0/F (where F0 depicts the fluorescence intensity without TC and F denotes the fluorescence intensity with TC) does not linearly correlate with the TC concentration (i.e. the value of quenching constant increases with the rising temperature), mechanistically, quenching is sensitive to the rate of molecular collision and can, therefore, be regarded as a dynamic in nature. The absorption spectra of TC, CDs, and CDs with TC were also examined (Fig. 5D) and did not show the appearance of new absorption peaks upon the addition of TC, which further establishes a dynamic quenching mechanism. Additionally, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to determine whether the quenching mechanism was DQ or SQ (Equation (3)) [49].
(3) I0/I = 1 + K [Q] = 1+ kqτ0 [Q]
where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities without and with a quencher, respectively, K represents quenching constant (Stern-Volmer), k q and Q denote quenching rate constant and the quencher concentration, respectively. And τ represents CDs fluorescence lifetime. A K of 20400 L mol⁻1 was used in these calculations. Accordingly, as K = kqτ0 and τ₀ was already calculated, kq was obtained as 1.4 × 1012 L mol⁻1s⁻1. Generally, quenching due to a diffusion-controlled process gives a kq value of approximately 1 × 101⁰ L mol⁻1s⁻1. The higher value of the kq obtained here indicates the contribution from a static mechanism [50] due to TC and CD binding [2].

To further investigate the mechanism, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels were measured via UV–Vis spectrophotometry and cyclic voltammetry (CV). The values were calculated using the empirical equations (4) and (5) as described previously in the literature [51].
(4) EHOMO (eV) = - (EOxidation+ 4.4)
(5) ELUMO (eV) = - (EReduction+ 4.4)

The values of the CD HOMO and LUMO were −7.78 and −4.36 eV, respectively (Fig. 5E). Furthermore, the value of Eg (the absorption edge in the optical data) is 3.42 eV, as calculated using the Tauc plot curve (Fig. S11). The HOMO and LUMO of TC were calculated as −6.2 and −3.87 eV, respectively, using the density functional hybrid procedure (B3LYP) in Dmol3 mode [46]. As represented in Fig. 5E, the CD electrons that are excited under the light cannot move from the CD LUMO to the TC LUMO as the energy level in the orbit of CDs is lower than TC. Hence, CDs and TC cannot contribute to the electron transfer in light. Hence, these results imply that a dual quenching mechanism, consisting of dynamic and static processes, enables the detection of TC. The dual quenching mechanism has also been reported in an earlier study, describing the interaction between a drug and bovine serum albumin [50]. Our findings are in contrast with earlier reported TC sensing, where the sensing mechanism was based on static quenching only [2,28]. This difference can be attributed to the use of Nigella sativa seeds as the raw material for the synthesis of CDs, as it does not require chemical additives. Noticeably, the material used for earlier sensors were chemically synthesized or green material with chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-Lys via fluorescence enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of L-Lys detection by CDs was done in the pH range of 2–11. As shown in Fig. S12, at pH above 5, the maximum intensity of CDs/L-Lys was observed. Thus, biological pH 7 was selected for conducting all experiments. L-Lys is an essential amino acid; which humans have to procure from food items. As the human body cannot synthesize L-Lys, the sensing of this indispensable amino acid above normal limits is desired [52]. To further evaluate the possible applications of Nigella sativa CDs, we assessed the sensing ability of CDs for L-Lys detection. Strikingly, the CD PL intensity was enhanced in a dose-dependent manner upon the addition of L-Lys (0–500 μM), suggesting that the CDs are sensitive to L-Lys (Fig. 6 A). Notably, no PL spectral shifts were observed due to L-Lys detection compared to the case of TC detection. Fig. 6B shows the relationship between the normalized CD PL intensity (I/I0) and L-Lys concentration, where I and I0 stand for PL intensities in the presence and absence of L-Lys, respectively. The PL intensity increased after adding L-Lys with a linear regression equation of I/I0 = 0.00066x + 1.02988 and correlation coefficient of 0.9990, indicating a linear correlation ranged from 0 to 500 μM. This is similar to recent studies that reported an enhancement in PL intensity after the addition of L-Lys [38]. Notably, the LOD for L-Lys detection is 453 nM, which is higher than that recently reported by Copur et al. [38].

In this research, CQDs were prepared using citric acid and C2H8N2. In contrast, the CDs used in the current study were prepared from a purely natural resource. Selective L-Lys detection by the as-prepared CDs was evaluated against other amino acids, namely glycine, valine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, serine, asparagine, glutamine, glutamic acid, arginine, histidine, alanine, methionine, threonine, proline, aspartic acid, tryptophan, and cysteine. As shown in Fig. 6C, the coexisting amino acids, when present at 10 mM concentrations, showed no interfering effects on L-Lys (at 100 μM) sensing by CDs. The CDs have high selectivity and sensitivity for L-Lys. Furthermore, serum samples spiked with L-Lys were used to determine the clinical analytical application of the CDs. The detection of L-Lys varied in the range of 98.9–100.3%, demonstrating the reliability and utility of the CD sensor to detect L-Lys (Table S4). These results indicate that the CD-based sensor can selectively detect L-Lys in real samples.

L-Lys is a cationic residue with a long (CH2)4NH3 + side chain. Many researches have documented the formation of hydrogen bonds (HBs), corresponding to an interaction between NH3 + and a carbonyl (-CO) group. As described above, structural investigations confirm the presence of many moieties on the CD surface. As-prepared CDs have a large surface to volume ratio with abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. These functional groups participate in HBs with –NH2 groups present in L-Lys (as shown in Fig. 7 ). The absorption spectra (Fig. 6D) shows a blue shift of the n–π* transition peak of CDs from 324 nm to 316 nm after the addition of L-Lys, which further indicates that there is a binding interaction between CDs and L-Lys [53]. Furthermore, it was reported that fluorescence enhancement by inter-HB is a rare occurrence [54]. However, it has been stated that the proximity of HBs can induce a fluorescence enhancement [54–56]. Inter-HBs are responsible for increasing the energy gap between S1 (ππ*) and S2 (nπ*) states and enhances the fluorescence [55,56]. Hence, the enhancement of fluorescence can be ascribed to the HB interactions between the CD surface moieties and L-Lys NH3 + (Fig. 7). The interaction between CDs and L-Lys restricts (intra-molecularly) rotation and vibration of functionalized units, leading to a reduction in the non-radiative relaxation of excited states [57–59]. At the same excitation wavelength, the CD-L-Lys conjugate showed a higher PL emission. To add further, as per recent data, among atoms (N, O, S, and P) with comparable bond strength; N is a stronger hydrogen bond-acceptor [60–62]. In addition, Møller et al. have shown that the strength of the hydrogen bond is strongly related to the N atom's hybridization (sp3 > sp2 > sp) condition, which increases by increasing the N lone pair's p-character [63]. The end of the L-Lys side chain has a protonated sp3-hybridized N atom [64]. Therefore, the selective detection of L-Lys could be ascribed to strong inter H-bonding with CDs compared to the remaining amino acids. Further, to get more insight into the mechanism of L-Lys detection, the zeta potential measurement was done. The zeta potential of CDs changed from −31.6 to −22.5 after the addition of L-Lys. This may be due to the HB between CDs and L-Lys, which consequently increased the PL intensity of CDs.

Compared to previously reported methods, the sensitivity, selectivity, and green synthesis approach using Nigella sativa seeds render the herein reported CDs unique. We applied the as-prepared CDs for the selective detection of TC and L-Lys. The PL intensity of CDs was suppressed in the presence of TC, while the addition of L-Lys led to an enhancement in CD fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, the mechanisms of the selective detection of the two analytes were discussed. It was found that both static and dynamic approaches, along with IFE were responsible for PL quenching of CDs and subsequent detection of TC. The introduction of L-Lys led to hydrogen-bonding interactions with the CD surface, leading to an increase in fluorescence intensity. Overall, new CDs have been developed for sensing of TC and L-Lys.

Neha Sharma: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Kyusik Yun: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (2017R1A2B4004700). This work was supported by the Gachon University research fund of 2019 (GCU-2019-0704).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
